text:
St Mary's Hospital 

Institute of Neurology 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Patel  
Riverside Health Centre  
12 Wharf Road, London N1 7RW  

Dear Dr Patel,  

Sophia Bennett, DOB: 14-05-1992, Hospital No: H483920 NHS No. 9083124476  
Mooring 5, Regent’s Canal, London, NW1 8AL 

Medication: 

Levetiracetam 750 mg twice daily. Patient-reported good daytime tolerability; occasional dizziness in the evenings. She uses a pill organiser and phone alarms for adherence. 

History (patient-reported): 

She lives on a houseboat/mobile home and reports intermittent power cuts that occasionally prevent timely charging of her phone and seizure-alert wearable. Since giving birth eight months ago, she describes a noticeable upswing in the number of brief events compared with her pre-pregnancy baseline. These typically occur in the late evening when fatigued and when childcare demands have been high. She has not required emergency care, and there have been no clear provoking illnesses or fevers. 

Events: 

- Short episodes described as sudden internal "rush" with blankness and loss of thread of conversation lasting 20–40 seconds, 2–3 times per week for the past six weeks (previously once every 2–3 weeks before the baby arrived).  
- One longer episode three weeks ago with lip-smacking and unresponsiveness for approximately 1–2 minutes, followed by confusion for about 10 minutes; witnessed by her partner.  
- No generalised convulsions reported. No injuries or tongue-biting. 

Contributors and context (patient-reported): 

- Sleep disruption due to infant care.  
- Missed evening dose on two occasions when power outage disabled phone reminders.  
- Increased psychosocial stress; no alcohol or recreational drugs.  
- Breastfeeding continues; no intercurrent infections. 

Examination (today): 

Alert and oriented. Speech fluent. Cranial nerves intact. Motor and coordination unremarkable. No focal neurological deficits on brief screen. 

Plan: 

- Continue levetiracetam 750 mg twice daily. We discussed adherence strategies that do not rely on mains power (wind-up/solar travel alarm; spare battery pack kept charged; paper dosing chart). She is comfortable to implement these immediately.  
- Provide written seizure first-aid advice for partner and guidance on when to call emergency services.  
- Safety: advised extra caution near water on the mooring and to avoid bathing alone; showers preferred.  
- Bloods: U&E, FBC, LFTs, vitamin D arranged locally.  
- Consider dose adjustment if brief events persist despite improved adherence and sleep measures; she will keep a seizure diary noting sleep duration, missed doses, and power outages.  
- Community support: suggested liaising with health visitor for night-feeding support to improve sleep; patient agrees. 

Follow-up: 

We will review her in three months, earlier if there is any further escalation in event frequency or any convulsive event. 

Yours sincerely

entities.text: 2–3 times per week for the past six weeks

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter describes current events consistent with focal seizures. Explicit frequency given: '2–3 times per week for the past six weeks' for short episodes. There was also a single longer episode three weeks ago (approximately 0.33 per week). Per the rule to select the highest frequency when multiple frequencies exist, the current seizure frequency is 2 to 3 per week. The patient is not seizure-free.
seizure_frequency_number: 2–3 times per week for the past six weeks

flaws.description: Gold (2 to 3 per week) disagrees with Reference (unknown)
